breaking_news.title: Interlock ransomware exploits Cisco firewall zero-day weeks before patch
breaking_news.summary:
The Interlock ransomware group actively exploited a critical zero-day vulnerability in Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center software weeks before a patch was released. This flaw, tracked as CVE-2026-20131, allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary Java code as root via insecure deserialization. Amazon Threat Intelligence confirmed the exploitation began in late January, turning an urgent patch into a zero-day emergency.

This incident highlights the severe risk of unpatched network infrastructure, as the vulnerability carries a maximum CVSS score of 10.0. CISA has added the flaw to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, urging immediate remediation across all affected Cisco firewall deployments. Organizations relying on Cisco FMC for perimeter security face significant exposure to ransomware deployment.

breaking_news.title: DarkSword iOS exploit kit uses multiple zero-days for state-sponsored attacks
breaking_news.summary:
Researchers have identified a new iOS exploit kit named DarkSword that leverages six vulnerabilities, including three zero-days, to achieve full device compromise. State-sponsored actors and commercial spyware vendors have deployed this chain against targets in Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Malaysia, and Ukraine since late 2025. The attack requires no user interaction beyond visiting a malicious website, enabling drive-by infections on iPhones running iOS 18.4 through 18.7.

The proliferation of DarkSword demonstrates a sophisticated capability to bypass Apple's security protections using a multi-stage exploit chain. Victims face total data theft and surveillance, with malware families like GHOSTBLADE deployed following successful compromise. This development underscores the escalating threat landscape for mobile devices used by high-value government and military targets.

breaking_news.title: Handala group wipes Stryker devices via Microsoft Intune compromise
breaking_news.summary:
Pro-Iranian hacktivist group Handala executed a destructive cyberattack on medical technology giant Stryker, wiping approximately 80,000 employee devices without using traditional malware. The attackers compromised Microsoft Intune, a cloud-based endpoint management service, to remotely erase data and disrupt operations across 79 countries. CISA has issued urgent warnings for organizations to harden their Intune configurations following this incident.

This attack represents a significant escalation in supply chain and infrastructure abuse, leveraging legitimate management tools to cause widespread operational disruption. The incident forced Stryker to shut down offices globally and manually process orders, impacting critical medical supply chains. Security leaders are now prioritizing phishing-resistant authentication to prevent similar endpoint management compromises.

breaking_news.title: GlassWorm supply chain campaign compromises 400+ GitHub and npm repositories
breaking_news.summary:
A coordinated supply chain campaign dubbed GlassWorm has targeted over 400 code repositories across GitHub, npm, and VSCode extensions. Attackers injected malicious payloads into popular packages and extensions, aiming to compromise developer environments and steal sensitive credentials. This widespread contamination affects the software development ecosystem, potentially impacting countless downstream applications and enterprises.

The campaign highlights the growing vulnerability of open-source software supply chains to sophisticated injection attacks. Developers and security teams must audit dependencies rigorously, as the malware operates stealthily within trusted development tools. This incident serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with third-party code integration in modern software development.

breaking_news.title: Critical unpatched Telnetd flaw enables unauthenticated root RCE
breaking_news.summary:
Security researchers disclosed a critical unpatched vulnerability in GNU InetUtils telnetd that enables unauthenticated remote code execution as root. Tracked as CVE-2026-32746, the flaw stems from a buffer overflow in the LINEMODE handler and carries a CVSS score of 9.8. Legacy infrastructure and embedded systems still running Telnet services are immediately at risk of full system compromise.

Although Telnet is largely replaced by SSH, the widespread presence of this daemon in older networking equipment creates a significant attack surface. A patch is expected by April 1, 2026, but administrators are urged to disable Telnet services immediately to mitigate risk. This vulnerability could allow attackers to take complete control of affected systems before authentication even occurs.

breaking_news.title: Ubiquiti patches critical UniFi flaw allowing account takeover
breaking_news.summary:
Ubiquiti patched two vulnerabilities in its UniFi Network app, including a maximum-severity flaw that allows attackers to hijack user accounts. The critical issue, CVE-2026-22557, involves a path traversal flaw that enables account takeover on versions 10.1.85 and earlier. This software is widely used to manage networking devices like access points, switches, and gateways in enterprise environments.

Successful exploitation could grant attackers full control over network infrastructure, allowing them to monitor traffic or disrupt connectivity. The vendor has released updates to address the maximum severity issue, urging all administrators to upgrade immediately. This flaw underscores the importance of maintaining management software to prevent unauthorized access to critical network hardware.

breaking_news.title: CISA adds SharePoint and Zimbra flaws to KEV catalog due to active exploitation
breaking_news.summary:
CISA added critical flaws in Microsoft SharePoint and Synacor Zimbra Collaboration Suite to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog due to active exploitation. The SharePoint vulnerability allows code execution via deserialization of untrusted data, while the Zimbra flaw involves a stored cross-site scripting issue. Both vulnerabilities are being actively abused by threat actors to compromise government and enterprise servers.

Federal agencies have been ordered to patch these systems immediately to prevent unauthorized access and data exfiltration. The active exploitation of these widely deployed collaboration tools poses a significant risk to organizations relying on them for internal communication. Security teams must prioritize patching to mitigate the threat of remote code execution and account takeover.

breaking_news.title: Feds disrupt IoT botnets behind massive DDoS attacks targeting DoD
breaking_news.summary:
The U.S. Justice Department, alongside Canadian and German authorities, dismantled infrastructure behind four major IoT botnets compromising over three million devices. These botnets, named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid, and Mossad, were responsible for record-smashing DDoS attacks capable of knocking major targets offline. The operation involved seizing domains and virtual servers used to launch hundreds of thousands of attacks.

This disruption significantly reduces the immediate threat of large-scale distributed denial-of-service attacks against critical infrastructure and government networks. The seized infrastructure was allegedly used to target Internet addresses owned by the Department of Defense and other high-value entities. The takedown demonstrates international cooperation in combating botnet-driven cybercrime and protecting internet stability.
